topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the worn carpet of my hallway, felt like a physical blow. I stared at the fine print detailing the impending conversion of our familiar brick residence into luxury units. My hand trembled slightly as I smoothed the paper flat on the worn oak table. I remember the sound of the mail slot clattering as I retrieved it, a mundane routine suddenly charged with a sharp, unpleasant tension. I walked back to my apartment, the silence amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. Every corner of the living room seemed to mock me with its familiar furniture, now just relics awaiting removal. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees to my chest, and let a slow, hot wave of resignation wash over me.

[story 2]

"Condo." The word echoed in the sterile air of the management office, each syllable landing like a stone in my stomach. I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the cheerful, corporate jargon masking the reality of what was happening to my home. When I finally stepped out onto the stoop, the afternoon sun felt abrasive on my skin. I gripped the strap of my bag so tightly my knuckles turned white. I didn't bother to look up at the building; instead, I focused intently on the pattern of cracks in the sidewalk, trying to anchor myself to something solid. A deep, hollow ache settled behind my ribs, a persistent dullness that made breathing feel shallow.

[story 3]

I found myself tracing the outline of the window frame, my fingertip lingering on the cool glass. The announcement had been delivered with such practiced indifference, a casual shrug that somehow managed to dismantle everything I thought I knew about this place. I stood by the balcony, the city noise a distant, indifferent roar beneath me. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I turned away from the view, focusing instead on the mundane task of watering the wilting potted plant on the sill. The water felt cold against my fingers, and I couldn't seem to muster the energy to move anything else.
